subject: [RFC PATCH 05/40] mm: page_alloc: remove watermark boost mechanism

2026-05-20.patch_summary: This patch removes the watermark boost mechanism from the Linux kernel's page allocation system. The mechanism, which was introduced to react to fragmentation events at the pageblock granularity, is no longer necessary due to changes in how superpageblocks handle anti-fragmentation. The boost would often cause unnecessary wakeups of kswapd even when there were plenty of free pages. The patch removes various related functions and data structures, including the ZONE_BOOSTED_WATERMARK flag and the watermark_boost_factor sysctl.

reviews.author: Usama Arif
reviews.summary:
The reviewer noted that removing the watermark boost mechanism makes the change userspace-visible, causing writes to /proc/sys/vm/watermark_boost_factor to return -ENOENT instead of being accepted. They suggested adding a Documentation/ABI/removed/ note explaining what replaces it and keeping the sysctl entry as a no-op with warning for a few cycles before deletion.

Reviewer noted that removing the watermark boost mechanism would also require updating the 'boost' field in /proc/zoneinfo and show_mem output, suggesting a similar change should be made for these as well.
reviews.raw_body:
This is a userspace-visible removal. Writes to
/proc/sys/vm/watermark_boost_factor will now return -ENOENT instead of being
accepted, breaking userspace.

I am not sure whats been done in the past, but we need to add a
Documentation/ABI/removed/ note explaining what replaces it (SPB steering).
and also maybe keep the sysctl entry as a no-op with warning for a few
cycles before deletion?

---

The boost: field in /proc/zoneinfo and the show_mem output also disappears.
Something similar might need to be done for this as well.

2026-05-26.patch_summary: This patch removes the watermark boost mechanism from the Linux kernel's page allocation code. The mechanism was introduced to react to fragmentation events at the pageblock granularity, but it has been found to cause issues in workloads with high memory availability. With the introduction of superpageblocks and anti-fragmentation enforcement at 1 GiB granularity, sub-pageblock fallbacks are no longer meaningful signals. The boost is also applied before the success path is decided, leading to unnecessary wakeups of kswapd even when free pages are abundant. The patch drops the entire mechanism, removing various functions, flags, and sysctls related to watermark boosting.
